<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: cognitive-compilation-and-semantic-ir
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/cognitive-compilation-and-semantic-ir.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/cognitive-compilation-and-semantic-ir.qmd@a57098412
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR

Plans do not become reliable artifacts just because they are scheduled. A plan
still has to cross the gap between "what should happen" and "what was actually
built." Cognitive compilation names that gap and gives it an engineering
surface.

The central idea is familiar from ordinary software compilers. Useful systems
rarely jump straight from human intention to machine behavior. They pass
through intermediate forms: source plans, typed records, dependency graphs,
lowering steps, validators, error messages, and repair loops. Advanced AI work
needs the same discipline. Code, prose, diagrams, schemas, proof targets, and
tool jobs should not appear as polished blobs with no visible lineage. They
should be lowered through addressable obligations.

Semantic IR is the artifact language for that lowering. It lets the stack say
what each unit of work is for, what it depends on, what it must preserve, which
validator can reject it, what authority it needs, and how far a repair is
allowed to reach. Once obligations have addresses, a failure can become local.
A broken requirement does not have to trigger a full regeneration of the whole
artifact. The system can inspect the atom, dependency, validator, lowering
receipt, or repair ledger that failed.

That is the reader promise of this chapter: generated work should be explainable
through the obligations it preserved. If the stack cannot trace a target output
back to source plan, semantic atoms, assumptions, validators, and residuals, it
has generated an artifact rather than compiled one.

## Problem

LLM workflows need intermediate representations that separate requirements,
plans, artifacts, repairs, and target runtimes.

Direct generation compresses too many jobs into a single act. A prompt or chat
turn asks the model to infer the user's real goal, preserve earlier constraints,
choose a structure, write the artifact, satisfy unstated validators, and repair
whatever breaks. Sometimes that works. But when the work becomes long,
high-risk, reused, reviewed, or changed by multiple agents, the absence of an
intermediate form becomes expensive.

The stack needs a place where intent stops being only prose and becomes a
structured build surface. Planning can decide what work should happen, but the
compiler layer must decide how that work is represented before it is executed.
The semantic IR gives downstream layers a way to ask simple questions: what is
this artifact preserving, what can reject it, what authority applies, and what
repair is allowed?

That separation matters because failure is normal. Generated work often fails
by omission, drift, hidden assumption, invalid dependency, stale context, or a
validator that was never made explicit. Without an intermediate representation,
the repair path is usually more prompting. With semantic IR, the repair path can
start at the failed obligation.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Generate-from-prompt workflows entangle requirements and implementation, drift
over long contexts, and make incremental repair fragile.

The `cognitive_compilation` source supplies the main pressure: complex
generative work should be staged as plan formation, semantic compilation,
target compilation, validation, and localized repair. PlanForge contributes the
scheduling view: work units need dependencies, quality gates, model/tool tiers,
and critical-path awareness. GenesisCode contributes provenance and obligation
discipline. TreeLLM contributes the representation intuition that structure
should be navigable outside model weights. VIEA connects the compiler to the
larger intent-to-artifact ledger.

External baselines sharpen the analogy without proving this stack. PDDL and
SHOP2 show why explicit action models and decomposition methods can matter.
Tree of Thoughts treats reasoning paths as inspectable candidates. DreamCoder
shows how reusable abstractions can change the search space. Those sources
make semantic IR easier to position, but this repository has not reproduced
planner results, program-synthesis results, or reasoning-search benchmarks.

Compiler infrastructure adds the stronger comparison. LLVM IR shows how an
intermediate representation can have typed structure, equivalent in-memory,
serialized, and human-readable forms, and verifier boundaries for analysis and
transformation. MLIR shows why modern compiler systems often need multiple
levels of representation, dialects, modular passes, verifiers, and progressive
lowering across targets. Translation validation supplies the safety lesson:
each lowering should be checked against its source relation after the
translation, not merely trusted because the compiler ran.

That is the discipline this chapter wants for cognitive artifacts. A generated
chapter section, schema, proof target, experiment, or job packet should not
just be produced. It should carry a source obligation, a target artifact, a
lowering receipt, and a validation boundary.

Prompting can remain the human input surface. It should not be the only
internal build artifact. A conversation transcript is too unstable to carry all
requirements, assumptions, dependencies, validators, authority limits, and
repair scopes by itself.

## Core Claim

Cognitive compilation should transform goals into semantic intermediate
representations that can be optimized, verified, repaired, and targeted.

The evidence boundary is architectural argument. The assigned source notes and
passage-reviewed mappings support discussion of semantic IR, compiler-style
boundaries, provenance, explicit semantic substrates, and artifact-ledger
integration. External notes ground program synthesis, compiler IR, multi-level
IR, and translation validation. They do not establish compiler correctness,
arbitrary source-plan parsing, target-lowering preservation,
localized-repair performance, artifact validator adequacy, LLVM or MLIR
integration, translation-validation proof, quality improvement, or cost
improvement in this repository.

## Mechanism

Cognitive compilation inserts a compiler boundary between a planned task and a
finished artifact. The boundary takes a source plan and lowers it into semantic
atoms. Those atoms form a DAG: each atom has a purpose, dependencies,
obligations, constraints, assumptions, authority requirements, validator state,
target hints, lowering state, repair scope, source references, residuals, and a
support-state effect.

The DAG is where other layers can participate. Planning can schedule the atoms.
Virtual context can supply the right evidence and memory. Execution can turn
target-ready atoms into jobs or artifacts. Verification can reject failed
validators. The artifact graph can record what was built and why. Claim ledgers
can record whether a repair changed an obligation.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Goal["Goal and command contract"] --> Plan["Source plan"]
  Plan --> IR["Semantic IR DAG"]
  IR --> Passes["Compiler passes: normalize, type, lint, schedule"]
  Passes --> Target["Target IR or job graph"]
  Target --> Artifact["Artifact"]
  Artifact --> Check{"Validator pass?"}
  Check -- "yes" --> Ledger["Artifact graph + claim ledger"]
  Check -- "no" --> Repair["Localized repair on failed IR nodes"]
  Repair --> Passes
  Ledger --> Boundary["Support boundary recorded"]
  Boundary --> Feedback["Reusable compiler feedback"]
```

**What the semantic compiler shows:** Work moves from goal to source plan,
then through semantic IR into a target artifact. Validator failure routes back
to the failed IR boundary instead of restarting the entire artifact by default.

The lowering receipt is the second key object. It records which semantic atoms
produced a target artifact, which obligations were preserved, which assumptions
were introduced, which validators passed or failed, and which residuals remain.
Without that receipt, a finished file can look credible while hiding the path
by which it forgot its requirements.

The compiler boundary should also make acceptance states explicit. A source
plan can be accepted or rejected. A semantic IR can be well formed or invalid.
A lowering pass can preserve obligations or break them. A target artifact can
pass or fail its validator. Treating those states as one vague "done" state is
how generation becomes evidence laundering.

## Interfaces

The interface is a typed semantic DAG, not a prose plan.

Planning owns graph formation and scheduling. The compiler owns obligation
preservation across lowering. Execution owns side effects and delivered
artifacts. Verification owns acceptance or rejection. The artifact graph owns
durable lineage. Claim ledgers own support-state and revision consequences.

A minimum semantic atom needs fields for identity, source plan, intent,
obligation references, obligation status, inputs, outputs, constraints,
dependencies, assumptions, authority, IR validity, validator status, target,
lowering state, target artifact, lowering receipt, repair scope, repair ledger,
source references, residuals, support-state effect, and non-claims.

The current public schema provides that record shape. It is useful because it
gives future traces a stable place to put obligations, assumptions, validator
state, lowering receipts, repair refs, residuals, and non-claims. It is not a
compiler. A record shape can tell later work where evidence belongs; it cannot
prove that arbitrary goals can be parsed into correct atoms or lowered into
valid artifacts.

## Invariants

- IR preserves declared requirements.
- Repair changes are localized and auditable.
- Target compilation records assumptions.
- Lowering receipts name obligations, validators, residuals, and introduced
  assumptions.
- A syntactically valid artifact cannot count as semantically accepted unless
  its required obligations remain visible.

Obligation non-erasure is the central invariant. A compiler pass may refine,
split, defer, reject, or explicitly revise an obligation. It may not silently
drop it. If an obligation no longer applies, the ledger needs a revision
reason rather than an absent edge.

Repair locality is the practical invariant. When a validator fails, the system
should identify the failed atom, dependency, target artifact, and repair scope.
If a repair changes a prior obligation, the claim ledger must record that the
build graph changed. Otherwise the final artifact can appear to satisfy the
original plan after the plan has quietly moved.

## Failure modes

- IR drift.
- Compiler hallucination.
- Repair that breaks earlier requirements.
- Semantic under-specification, where the graph is well typed but omits what
  the human actually cared about.
- Validator scarcity, where prose, diagrams, or policy claims lack checks that
  can reject bad lowering.
- Compilation overhead, where the IR layer costs more than the task deserves.
- Compilation laundering, where a syntactic schema pass is mistaken for
  obligation preservation.

The hardest failure is not a malformed record. The hardest failure is a clean
record that represents the wrong obligation. That is why source-plan quality,
validator adequacy, and reviewable lowering receipts matter as much as schema
validity.

Compilation should therefore be selective. Small low-risk tasks may not need
the full compiler path. Long-lived, high-risk, reusable, multi-agent, or
repair-heavy artifacts do. The IR layer earns its cost when it prevents hidden
requirement loss, makes repair local, or gives reviewers a clear line from
artifact back to obligation.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is not a general cognitive compiler. It is
a hand-authored semantic-atom trace for one artifact type, plus a compile,
verify, fail, and repair loop around that trace.

The first useful slice could take a small source plan, lower it into a semantic
atom DAG, produce one target artifact, attach one lowering receipt, run one
passing validator, run one failing validator, and demonstrate one localized
repair that updates the repair ledger without regenerating unrelated work. The
target artifact could be a chapter section, a protocol schema, a small code
file, or a proof-target stub.

The repository already has a semantic atom schema shape, finite Lean predicates
over obligation preservation and repair-ledger gates, synthetic plan-execution
checks that touch requirement preservation, and a small hand-authored trace
harness for source-plan obligations, lowering receipts, target audits, and
repair scope. That is record and fixture coverage. It does not yet include a
source-plan parser, target-lowering engine, concrete artifact validator suite,
localized-repair benchmark, or quality/cost comparison against direct
generation.

## Beyond the State of the Art

In mature form, cognitive compilation becomes a build system for cognitive work.
Goals, command contracts, plans, sources, authority limits, validators, and
context requirements lower into semantic IR before producing code, prose,
schemas, proof targets, experiments, or tool jobs.

The mature product surface is not only a generator. It is a compiler workbench
that shows the obligation graph behind every important artifact. A reviewer can
ask why a section exists, which source plan created it, what it must preserve,
which validator accepted it, what assumption was introduced, which residual is
open, and what downstream artifact must rebuild if the obligation changes.

The mature toolchain would include source-plan language, typed semantic atoms,
dependency analysis, validator routing, target backends, lowering receipts,
localized repair, artifact graph integration, claim-ledger integration, and
rebuild logic. It would know when a small direct-generation path is cheaper and
when risk, reuse, or repair burden requires the full compiler boundary.

That endpoint is a target architecture, not a current result. The present
support boundary remains architectural argument until compiler traces, lowering
rules, validators, repair-locality tests, target backends, negative controls,
and benchmark comparisons show that the stack can rebuild cognitive artifacts
while keeping semantic obligations addressable.

## Summary

Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR gives the stack a compiler boundary
between intention and artifact. It changes the question from "did the model
produce something plausible?" to "which obligations did this artifact preserve,
which validators accepted it, and where can repair safely happen?"

The idea is powerful because it makes partial correctness visible. A generated
artifact can be wrong in many ways, but an obligation graph can show where the
wrongness entered: source plan, semantic atom, dependency, assumption,
validator, lowering receipt, or repair ledger.

The present evidence remains deliberately narrow. The chapter has source
grounding, a public semantic-atom record shape, finite Lean gates, synthetic
requirement-preservation checks, and a hand-authored trace harness that rejects
missing receipts, dropped obligations, global repair, and syntactic passes that
do not preserve obligations. It does not yet have a working cognitive compiler.
The next path is to connect the same control surface to concrete target
artifacts, real validators, and measured localized repair before any broader
compiler claim moves.

## Handoff

Semantic IR can only preserve obligations when the context feeding those
obligations is addressable, authorized, and reviewable. Virtual Context ABI
defines that memory boundary. It gives compiled work stable context handles,
versions, mounts, snapshots, representation contracts, adequacy signals, and
fault behavior so semantic atoms do not inherit invisible memory or stale
evidence.
